flashcards.Back: The process in the WLC GUI where an administrator navigates to WLANs > Create New to define a unique profile name and SSID for a wireless network.
An administrative label used to identify a specific WLAN configuration within the WLC. it is internal to the controller and not seen by wireless clients.
The actual text name of the wireless network that is broadcast to clients. it must be carefully chosen as it is the name users will select to connect.
A unique numerical identifier (1–512) assigned to a WLAN within the WLC. it is used to track and manage the wireless network entry in the controller's database.
The main configuration screen in the WLC GUI where you enable the WLAN, set the SSID, and map the WLAN to a specific network interface.
A critical checkbox in the WLC GUI. even if perfectly configured, a WLAN will not broadcast until it is explicitly enabled.
The setting in the WLC GUI that binds a specific WLAN to a logical interface, which in turn maps the wireless traffic to a physical VLAN ID.
The GUI section where administrators define Layer 2 and Layer 3 security policies, such as WPA2 encryption and client authentication methods.
The most common security scheme selected in the WLC drop-down menu, providing robust encryption for modern enterprise and personal wireless networks.
The hardware-accelerated encryption standard selected under WPA2 parameters to provide maximum data confidentiality for wireless traffic.
An authentication key management setting used for WPA2-Personal, where all clients use the same static password to join the wireless network.
An authentication key management option for WPA2-Enterprise that requires clients to authenticate against an external RADIUS server.
The GUI section used to prioritize wireless traffic. it allows administrators to assign a specific quality of service level to all traffic on a WLAN.
The highest priority level in the WLC GUI, typically reserved for time-critical voice traffic (VoIP).
A high-priority QoS level used for video-on-demand and other time-sensitive multimedia streaming.
The default QoS level in the WLC GUI, used for standard 'Best Effort' data traffic like web browsing and email.
The lowest priority QoS level, typically assigned to background traffic or guest wireless networks to prevent them from impacting critical business data.
A GUI section containing specialized parameters like session timeouts, client load balancing, and interference mitigation settings.
An advanced setting that temporarily blocks a client MAC address after repeated failed authentication attempts to protect against brute-force attacks.
An automated feature configured in the WLC GUI that identifies areas where client signal quality is too low and prompts the WLC to increase AP transmit power.
A security setting that prevents wireless clients on the same WLAN from communicating directly with each other, forcing all traffic to the wired network.
An advanced setting that limits the total number of simultaneous associations permitted on a single WLAN to prevent AP overload.
A checkbox in the WLC GUI that determines if the SSID is included in beacon frames. Disabling this creates a 'hidden' network.
A WLAN setting that defines which radio bands (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, or both) will support the wireless network.
The maximum duration a client can stay connected to a WLAN before being forced to re-authenticate.
A mandatory QoS requirement for high-speed 802.11n/ac/ax networks that ensures the AP and client can negotiate priority levels.
A WLC feature that proactively encourages dual-band clients to connect to the less congested 5 GHz radio instead of 2.4 GHz.
An advanced setting that distributes clients across multiple nearby APs to ensure no single AP becomes a performance bottleneck.
A GUI option that enables rapid roaming for mobile clients, reducing the handoff time between access points in an ESS.
A menu section providing tools to monitor real-time client statistics, AP radio status, and system error logs for troubleshooting.
